methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series B → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.9x) to $100M total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.skip_reason: insufficient narrow peer set in Housing & Construction (found 0 same-stage, 1 adjacent-stage, 0 similar-tier; need ≥3)
methods.rationale: 1 same-sector peers exist in Housing & Construction, but none of the narrow filters (same-stage / adjacent-stage / similar-valuation-tier) yielded ≥3 datapoints. Falling back to all-sector median would be misleading.

data.narrative:
Cover is a Series B-stage company in Housing & Construction that has raised over $100M to date. The valuation landscape for this sector is thin: the identifiable comp universe consists of only 1 private peer (ICON) with no public comparables. This scarcity of same-stage, same-sector transaction data significantly constrains the application of traditional peer-median methods.

Given the limited comp set, stage-progression analysis emerges as the primary applicable method. Using Cover's $100M total raised as a proxy base value and applying a 1.9x step-up multiplier typical for Series B progression yields an implied valuation of $190M. A peer-median approach was not executable due to insufficient datapoints—fewer than the three-company minimum needed for statistical reliability within the Housing & Construction sector at comparable stages.

The triangulated range derived from this single-method analysis spans $162M (low) to $218M (high), with a median of $190M. This range reflects sensitivity analysis around the stage-progression estimate rather than independent method convergence. Key uncertainties include the representativeness of the 1.9x multiplier for Cover's specific profile and the absence of recent Housing & Construction comparable transactions at similar valuations. Investors should view this as a preliminary estimate pending either additional sector comps or company-specific financial metrics (revenue, growth rate, path to profitability) that would support revenue or DCF-based approaches.
